1110000002.name: Hamad A. Al- Manea Trade Co.

finance.headline: Zero overdue, A+ grade, sustained 0% OVD — minor DSO overhang vs. 90d credit term, but peak season loading argues for a credit limit step-up.
finance.analysis:
AR Health: Balance of SAR 10.35M is 100% current. All aging buckets — 0-30d, 30-60d, 60d+ — are zero. OVD has held flat at 0.0% across six consecutive daily snapshots (Apr 29 – May 4), with no deterioration signal. This is the cleanest AR profile in the OR channel cohort and fully consistent with an A+ / Q1-Safe classification.

Collection Risk: Low, with one structural note to flag. YTD DSO of 113d and T12M DSO of 126d both exceed the contractual credit term of 90d by 23–36 days, indicating a habitual payment pattern that stretches modestly beyond terms. However, both figures sit well inside LG's DPO ceiling of 180d, and the account's 0% OVD is a sharp outperformance vs. the 35.4% sector OVD baseline. The 1.1x buy pace YTD vs. T12M confirms the account is actively purchasing into 2026 rather than pulling back — a positive signal for ongoing engagement. The 2026 sell-in of SAR 11.4M (through early May) will ramp materially as peak season (May–September) accelerates orders.

Credit Policy Rationale: With zero overdue, A+ standing, and the Saudi AC demand peak immediately ahead, the dominant risk is not over-exposure — it is under-allocation. A static credit limit will compress this account's order capacity precisely when sell-through velocity is highest, creating unnecessary friction with a proven payer. The DSO stretch is a pattern to monitor (particularly if YTD DSO approaches 130d or any aging bucket turns non-zero), but it has not materialized into overdue and does not justify a hold. A 20–30% credit limit increase is appropriate to support peak season loading.
finance.credit_rationale: Zero overdue, A+ grade, and imminent May-Sep peak demand create the right conditions to expand headroom and capture OR channel volume without collection risk.
action: Approve credit limit increase of 20–30% effective May 2026, ahead of peak season order cycle
Set DSO early-warning trigger: flag for review if YTD DSO exceeds 130d or any aging bucket turns non-zero
Initiate discussion on formalizing 120d payment terms to align documentation with actual payment behavior and reduce technical-overdue classification risk
finance.risk_reason: OVD 0.0% sustained, A+ grade, all aging buckets zero — DSO modestly above credit term but well within 180d DPO; no material collection risk.

sales.headline: Top-tier OR account with pristine AR but structural sell-in erosion (-19.9% YoY) demanding aggressive peak-season intervention before Q2 window closes.
sales.analysis:
Sell-in has declined from SAR 58.3M (2024) to SAR 46.7M (2025), a 19.9% contraction that cannot be attributed to seasonal drift alone. At SAR 11.4M YTD entering May, the account is tracking below 2025 pace in absolute terms — yet the 1.1x buy pace vs T12M average signals that recent order momentum has quietly stabilized. The most plausible root-cause hypotheses are: (1) inventory overhang from 2024 peak build-up suppressing 2025 orders, now partially worked down; (2) partial SKU shift toward competitor brands at floor level, not yet fully reversed; (3) demand-side softness in Al-Manea's sub-distribution network. Without sell-out data, hypothesis 2 carries the highest risk and warrants direct floor checks.

AR profile is best-in-class: five consecutive days of 0% OVD, balance actively reducing from SAR 10.3M to SAR 9.69M, and YTD DSO improving to 113d vs T12M baseline of 126d — all within the 90d credit term context for the region. There is zero financial stress signal here. Grade A+ designation and Q1-Safe quadrant are fully justified. The account is paying cleanly and buying at a slightly above-average pace, which is the key green flag heading into peak season.

Peak season (May–September) is opening now, and this is the highest-leverage intervention window. Al-Manea's A+ status and OR channel footprint make it a multiplier account — floor wins here ripple through sub-dealers. The structural sell-in decline is real, but the clean payment track record, stabilizing buy pace, and timing alignment mean the right move is active investment to re-anchor LG's share before Samsung or mid-tier Chinese brands consolidate position during the peak cycle.
sales.investment_rationale: Peak season entry point + stabilizing buy pace + pristine AR creates a high-ROI window to recover sell-in trajectory; credit headroom can unlock volume that 90d terms may currently be throttling.
action: Conduct floor audit at Al-Manea's top 3 showrooms before May 15 — verify LG display share vs Samsung/Gree and identify whether SKU mix gap (e.g., missing mid-tier Split lineup) is driving sell-in softness
Propose peak-season sell-out support package (installer co-op, display incentive, consumer promo) tied to minimum buy-in commitment for May–July; use 1.1x buy pace as the base target floor

Review credit limit vs current 90d outstanding (SAR 10.35M) — if limit is constraining peak-season ordering, recommend a temporary Q2/Q3 credit line increase contingent on continued 0% OVD performance
sales.channel_reason: Structural 2-year sell-in decline unresolved, but financial health is pristine and buy pace shows early recovery signal heading into peak season.

synthesis.headline: AR is flawless — zero overdue, A+ grade — but a two-year sell-in contraction of SAR 11.6M (-19.9% YoY) is the real threat; peak season opens NOW and is the last high-leverage window to reverse floor share loss before it becomes permanent.

verdict.one_line: Risk GREEN — INVEST. Pristine payer with a structural volume gap that only aggressive peak-season activation can close.

summary_3lines: What is happening: Al-Manea holds SAR 10.35M in fully current AR with zero overdue across all aging buckets, yet sell-in has contracted from SAR 58.3M (2024) → SAR 46.7M (2025) → SAR 11.4M YTD May 2026, tracking below 2025 pace in absolute terms — though 1.1x buy pace vs T12M signals early stabilization.

Why: Most probable cause is a 2024 inventory overhang suppressing 2025 orders, now partially cleared — but the higher-risk and unconfirmed hypothesis is SKU-level floor share erosion to Samsung or Chinese tier (Gree/Midea); without sell-out data this is the single largest intelligence gap.

What it means if we do nothing: Peak season (May–September) will run without counter-activation; competitors consolidate display and installer loyalty inside Al-Manea's sub-dealer network; the SAR 11.6M sell-in gap hardens from a cyclical dip into a structural loss of an A+ account.
synthesis.decision_required: Approve a 20–30% credit limit increase effective this week AND commit to a floor audit + peak-season sell-out support package by May 15 — the first peak-cycle orders are placed in May; missing this window means the Q2/Q3 volume is already allocated elsewhere.

options.option: Option A — Full Peak Investment: Approve credit increase now + deploy floor audit team to top 3 Al-Manea showrooms by May 15 + propose tied sell-out support package (installer co-op, display incentive, consumer promo) with minimum buy-in commitment for May–July.
options.consequence: Maximizes probability of recovering sell-in trajectory during the only high-leverage window of the year; credit headroom removes order friction for a proven zero-overdue payer; floor audit either confirms LG share is intact (reassuring) or surfaces competitor penetration early enough to counter-act before season peaks.

options.option: Option B — Hold and Monitor: Maintain current credit terms, wait for sell-out data before committing sell-out support budget, no floor intervention this cycle.
options.consequence: Preserves short-term opex but cedes peak-season floor and installer relationships to competitors; if the sell-in decline is share-driven (not inventory-driven), a second consecutive weak peak season will accelerate loss and make recovery in 2027 materially harder and more expensive.

timeline.step: Approve 20–30% credit limit increase for Al-Manea — enable peak-season order capacity without friction for a zero-overdue A+ account

Deploy field team for floor audit at Al-Manea's top 3 showrooms — verify LG vs Samsung/Gree display share and SKU gap (especially mid-tier Split lineup); output: share % by brand + specific model gap list
Present peak-season sell-out support proposal to Al-Manea — installer co-op + display incentive tied to May–July minimum buy-in commitment; use 1.1x T12M buy pace as baseline floor target

finance.headline: Grade-A account in solid shape — minor 30-60d blip against a clean OVD trend, but DSO structurally sits 43 days above credit term and needs watching as buy pace accelerates into peak season.
finance.analysis:
AR health is broadly positive. Current overdue of SAR 151,688 (2.0%) sits entirely in the 30-60d bucket with zero 60d+ exposure, and six consecutive daily snapshots through May 4 showed 0.0% OVD — meaning this balance surfaced within the last 24 hours and is likely a routine timing lag rather than a structural payment failure. Against a YTD peer baseline of 35.4% average overdue, 2.0% is exceptional, and the Grade A classification is well-supported.

The DSO picture deserves more scrutiny, however. YTD DSO of 133d is 43 days beyond the 90d credit term, and T12M DSO landing exactly at the DPO ceiling of 180d indicates the account is habitually paying at the outer boundary of LG's tolerance — not in breach, but with no buffer. The 1.4x accelerated buy pace in 2026 (entering May peak season) will mechanically push AR balance higher; if payment cadence does not improve alongside volume, DSO could breach DPO before Q3 is out. Sell-in has declined YoY (2024: SAR 19M → 2025: SAR 13.5M → 2026 YTD: SAR 7.1M), so the acceleration signal likely reflects seasonal front-loading rather than a structural recovery — needs confirmatory sell-thru data.

For credit policy, the account does not warrant a decrease — payment behavior is clean and the overdue is negligible. An increase is premature given T12M DSO sitting flush against DPO 180d; expanding the credit ceiling before DSO demonstrates a downward trend would simply extend LG's exposure without a safety margin. Maintaining the current limit while tracking DSO weekly through the May–June peak is the prudent call. If DSO pulls back toward 120d by end of June and sell-thru velocity justifies incremental orders, a mid-season limit review would be appropriate.
finance.credit_rationale: T12M DSO at DPO ceiling (180d) leaves no safety buffer — credit expansion is premature until payment cadence demonstrates a clear downward trend.
action: Clear SAR 151,688 (30-60d) within this week — call the account, confirm payment date, and log commitment in CRM before it ages into the 60d+ bucket.
Set a DSO tripwire: if YTD DSO exceeds 150d by end of May (driven by accelerating buy pace), escalate to credit committee for limit review.

Request sell-thru data for April–May to validate whether 1.4x buy pace reflects genuine consumer demand pull or inventory accumulation — this determines whether a mid-peak credit increase is warranted.
finance.risk_reason: 2.0% OVD well below 35.4% baseline, zero 60d+ exposure, and clean recent payment trend — DSO at DPO ceiling is a watch item, not an immediate risk.

sales.headline: Peak-season front-loading confirmed with 1.4x accelerated buy pace and near-zero AR risk — 2025 trough is behind us, 2026 recovery is live.
sales.analysis:
Channel health is recovering decisively. The 2024→2025 Sell-In collapse (-29% YoY: SAR 19M → SAR 13.5M) most likely reflected an inventory overhang cycle — the account over-ordered in 2024, spent 2025 digesting stock, and is now re-entering buy mode aggressively. The 1.4x buy pace vs T12M average, with YTD already at SAR 7.1M by early May, suggests full-year 2026 Sell-In could approach SAR 18–20M if peak season (May–September) executes as the current pace implies. Without direct sell-out data the inference is directional, but the timing — accelerating exactly at peak season onset — is the right pattern for a healthy OR account.

AR quality has improved dramatically and is one of the cleanest signals in this assessment. YTD DSO has compressed from 180d (T12M) to 133d — a 47-day improvement — and the OVD rate sits at 2.0% against a 35.4% YTD baseline. The six-day streak of 0% overdue prior to today's snapshot reinforces that payments are running ahead of historical norms. The sole overdue bucket (SAR 151,688 in 30–60d) is immaterial at 2% of balance and shows no 60d+ tail risk. The account's financial posture is not a constraint on investment — it is an argument for it.

The convergence of improving payment behavior, accelerating buy momentum, and peak-season timing creates a clear investment window. The risk is inaction: a Grade A OR account recovering from a trough, buying 40% above historical pace, heading into the highest-demand months of the Saudi AC calendar is exactly the profile where targeted sell-out support and modest credit headroom can amplify volume disproportionately. The 2025 decline was structural (inventory digestion), not a relationship or competitive defection signal.
sales.investment_rationale: 1.4x buy pace acceleration at peak season entry, combined with near-zero AR risk and a DSO trajectory compressing 47 days YoY, justifies incremental credit and sell-out activation to capture the full recovery upside.
action: Propose a credit line review — current 90d term with 133d YTD DSO and 2% OVD is a conservative setup; a structured 30-day extension or incremental limit increase would unlock volume headroom entering peak season
Deploy sell-out support package (in-store display refresh, installer incentive, consumer promotion) within the next 2 weeks before peak demand materializes in June–July

Schedule a May mid-month sell-through check — since there is no sell-out data feed, request a direct stock-on-hand report from the account to validate whether the 1.4x buy pace reflects consumer pull or is rebuilding inventory buffer
sales.channel_reason: Buy pace 1.4x above T12M baseline, DSO improving 47 days YoY, OVD at 2% vs 35.4% historical average — all leading indicators pointing toward a healthy, accelerating account entering peak season.

synthesis.headline: Grade-A account in confirmed recovery: 47-day YoY DSO improvement and 1.4x buy pace entering peak season make this an INVESTMENT candidate — deploy sell-out support now, unlock credit after one more DSO checkpoint before June peak commitment.

verdict.one_line: Risk GREEN — Invest. DSO compressing fast, OVD negligible; activate sell-out support immediately and set a conditional credit trigger at end-of-May DSO review.

summary_3lines: Alsheta & Alsaif is recovering from a 2025 inventory-digestion trough: 1.4x buy pace with SAR 7.1M YTD by early May points toward an SAR 18–20M full-year run-rate if peak season executes.

AR quality is the strongest argument for investment — DSO compressed 47 days YoY (T12M 180d → YTD 133d), OVD sits at 2% against a 35.4% baseline, and six consecutive days of 0% overdue confirm the 30-60d blip is a timing lag, not a structural failure.

Holding the current conservative credit posture through peak season means a Grade-A account on a confirmed recovery trajectory hits its order ceiling mid-June, throttles buy pace, and risks shifting incremental volume to competitors willing to offer better terms.
synthesis.decision_required: Approve sell-out support deployment this week AND set a conditional credit increase trigger: if YTD DSO ≤ 140d at the May 31 checkpoint AND sell-thru data confirms consumer pull (not pure inventory rebuild), raise credit limit 20–30% for the June–August peak window.

options.option: Option A — Invest now: sell-out support + conditional credit gate for June
options.consequence: Full peak season upside captured; full-year Sell-In trajectory toward SAR 18–20M. Incremental credit exposure is manageable given 2% OVD and a compressing DSO trend. Key downside: if sell-thru stalls and account is inventory-loading, post-peak DSO could spike — mitigated by the mandatory May 15 sell-thru checkpoint before any credit decision is made.

options.option: Option B — Hold credit, sell-out support only, revisit in July
options.consequence: Lower credit risk, but account likely hits order ceiling during peak demand weeks (June–July). Probable full-year outcome: SAR 12–14M — recovery happens but sub-maximizes. Competitor with more flexible credit terms captures incremental volume from a Grade-A OR account during the highest-demand months of the Saudi AC calendar.

timeline.step: Call account, confirm payment on SAR 151,688 overdue (30-60d), log commitment in CRM before it ages into 60d+ bucket

Deploy sell-out support package (display refresh, installer incentive, consumer promotion) AND request stock-on-hand report to confirm whether 1.4x buy pace is consumer-pull or inventory buffer rebuild
DSO gate review: if YTD DSO ≤ 140d AND sell-thru confirms consumer pull, approve 20–30% credit limit increase for June–August peak window; if DSO > 150d, set tripwire for credit committee escalation

finance.headline: Saudi Bin Hamood is operating as a structural credit risk — DSO at 2.65x its credit term with SAR 34.4M aged beyond 60 days, while accelerating 2026 purchases without clearing legacy overdue.
finance.analysis:
AR health is severely impaired. The 60d+ bucket alone (SAR 34,368,695) represents 51.7% of the total balance — this is not a temporary timing mismatch but entrenched unpaid debt. YTD DSO of 318 days against a 120-day credit term means LG is effectively extending 2.65x the agreed financing. T12M DSO of 408 days indicates this is not a 2026 anomaly but a multi-year pattern. The small OVD improvement from 60.7% to 54.7% on April 30 reflects a one-time payment, not a structural collection recovery — OVD has been flat for five consecutive days since, meaning no follow-on collection momentum.

The revenue trajectory compounds the risk signal. Sell-In dropped 44% from 2024 to 2025, and 2026 YTD pace implies further absolute decline despite a 1.3x buy acceleration vs. T12M average. This combination — shrinking annual revenue but faster recent purchasing — is a red flag pattern: the account may be front-loading peak-season inventory on LG's balance sheet without demonstrating the cash flow capacity to retire aging debt. LG's exposure is growing directionally while collection lags severely.

On credit policy: decreasing the credit limit is the only defensible action. Maintaining or increasing exposure at 318-day DSO and 54.7% OVD — heading into peak season when the account will seek maximum new orders — would allow the 60d+ overdue mountain to grow unchecked. The A+ grade classification should be formally reviewed; current financial behavior is inconsistent with that designation. Any new shipment approval must be conditioned on documented collection milestones against the 60d+ bucket, not granted freely on account grade alone.
finance.credit_rationale: DSO at 2.65x credit term with 51.7% of balance aged 60d+ and no post-payment collection momentum makes further credit extension indefensible entering peak season.
action: Immediate credit hold on new orders — release only upon written payment plan committing SAR 10M+ reduction in 60d+ bucket within 30 days, with weekly tranches
Escalate to Credit Committee: formally downgrade account grade from A+ and set hard credit ceiling at current balance until DSO falls below 180d
For any peak-season orders approved by exception, require 30–50% advance payment (T/T) to reduce net credit exposure before goods issue
finance.risk_reason: DSO 318d vs 120d term, SAR 34.4M in 60d+ bucket (51.7% of balance), flat OVD trend post-payment, and 1.3x buy acceleration with no proportional collection — textbook credit stress entering high-volume season.

sales.headline: A+ large IR account accelerating purchases 1.3x into peak season while carrying SAR 34M in 60d+ overdue — buy-side momentum masked by severe financial stress.
sales.analysis:
Sell-in has collapsed two consecutive years: SAR 111.7M (2024) → SAR 62.3M (2025) → SAR 26.1M YTD 2026. However, the 1.3x buy pace versus T12M average is a genuine counter-signal; annualised it implies ~SAR 78–80M, a partial recovery. The timing matters: purchases are accelerating directly into the May–September peak window, which could reflect real consumer demand pull-through in the retail network. Without sell-out velocity data, though, we cannot distinguish genuine channel demand from inventory front-loading ahead of an anticipated credit clamp.

The financial structure is the dominant risk. Of SAR 66.5M outstanding, 54.7% is overdue, and 92% of that overdue bucket (SAR 34.4M) is already 60d+. YTD DSO of 318d is 2.6x the stated 120d credit term; the T12M DSO of 408d shows this is not a short-term aberration. The Apr 29→30 OVD improvement (60.7%→54.7%) coincided with a balance increase (SAR 64.8M→SAR 66.5M), meaning a partial payment was made but new shipments simultaneously loaded on — net exposure barely moved. Since May 1, balance has been flat: no payments, no new orders. The account is in stasis.

The paradox of an A+ account in Q3-Critical with a 1.3x buy pace suggests the account is using LG credit as primary working capital rather than as a transactional tool. This is a high-risk posture for LG: continued supply without collection milestones deepens the 60d+ exposure during the exact season when settlement pressure should be highest. The channel relationship has value, but that value cannot justify widening an already distressed AR position without concrete repayment commitment.
sales.investment_rationale: Freeze incremental credit and co-investment until OVD falls below 40% and a binding installment schedule on the SAR 34.4M 60d+ bucket is signed — then reassess sell-out support for late peak season.
action: Immediately engage account leadership to secure a written installment plan for the SAR 34.4M 60d+ overdue block — minimum SAR 10M collected by end of May as condition for continued supply
Suspend new shipments or convert to cash-before-delivery until OVD% drops below 40%; communicate this as policy, not punishment, to preserve the relationship

Obtain sell-out (POS) data from the account's retail network urgently — if consumer off-take is strong, use it as leverage to motivate collection (replenishment is available once receivables normalize)
sales.channel_reason: DSO 318d at 2.6x credit term, 60d+ overdue SAR 34M with no May payments received — financial stress overrides the 1.3x buy-pace positive signal.

synthesis.headline: Saudi Bin Hamood is using LG's balance sheet as working capital — SAR 34.4M aged beyond 60 days with zero May payments while accelerating new orders into peak season. Supply must stop until a binding installment plan is signed.

verdict.one_line: Risk RED — Hold. A+ grade masks a multi-year structural collection failure; 1.3x buy acceleration into peak season without clearing 60d+ debt deepens LG's exposure at the worst possible moment.

summary_3lines: SAR 34.4M (51.7% of total balance) is sitting in the 60d+ bucket with no payments received since April 30 — DSO at 318 days is 2.65x the agreed credit term, a pattern confirmed across T12M at 408 days, not a seasonal blip.

The account is front-loading peak-season inventory on LG credit without demonstrating cash flow capacity to retire aging debt — sell-in has collapsed 77% from 2024 to 2026 YTD while overdue entrenched above 50%, meaning revenue shrinkage and collection deterioration are moving in the same direction simultaneously.

If we ship through peak season without a binding repayment commitment, the 60d+ bucket will absorb another SAR 20–30M of new exposure by September — and by then, with no seasonal urgency remaining, the leverage to collect disappears entirely.
synthesis.decision_required: Will LG continue supplying Saudi Bin Hamood on existing credit terms — or enforce a formal installment plan on the SAR 34.4M 60d+ block as the non-negotiable condition for peak-season shipment release?

options.option: Option A — Enforce: Credit hold now, release conditioned on signed SAR 10M/month installment plan
options.consequence: Short-term supply disruption and relationship friction; but LG retains negotiating leverage before peak demand, reduces 60d+ exposure within 60–90 days, and signals that A+ grade carries accountability. If account refuses, the risk profile is confirmed and exit planning begins with eyes open.

options.option: Option B — Continue supply without condition through peak season
options.consequence: 60d+ bucket grows to an estimated SAR 50–65M by September; post-peak leverage collapses; DSO likely crosses 400d on YTD basis; the A+ grade classification becomes a liability that distorts the broader portfolio risk view. Recovery at that scale requires legal escalation.

timeline.step: Immediate credit hold — freeze new order processing; notify account that shipment release requires written installment commitment on SAR 34.4M 60d+ block (minimum SAR 10M by May 31, weekly tranches thereafter)

Escalate to Credit Committee: formally review A+ grade classification, set hard credit ceiling at current balance (SAR 66.5M), and approve exception process requiring 30–50% T/T advance for any peak-season shipment approved above ceiling

Demand sell-out (POS) data from account's retail network — if consumer off-take is strong, use replenishment availability as leverage to accelerate collection; if sell-out is weak, the 1.3x buy acceleration is confirmed inventory front-loading and exit planning should begin

finance.headline: Alkhunaizan holds SAR 1.13M in 60d+ overdue with zero 2026 purchases — collection risk is acute and credit exposure must be frozen immediately.
finance.analysis:
AR health is severely deteriorated: 62% of the SAR 1.84M balance is overdue, and 99.2% of that overdue (SAR 1,131,334) sits in the 60d+ aging bucket. This is not a timing variance — it is a structural collection failure. The 0–30d bucket is negligible at SAR 9,340, the 30–60d bucket is zero, meaning no fresh overdue is accumulating because no new transactions are occurring. The OVD rate has been flat at 62% for six consecutive days with the total balance barely moving, confirming no meaningful payments are incoming and no self-healing is underway.

The single most critical signal is zero 2026 Sell-In (SAR -193,490, net returns). An account that posted SAR 10.1M in 2025 — 67% YoY growth — has made no purchases in 2026 at the start of the AC peak season. This strongly suggests either LG has already placed an informal shipment block, or the account is itself in financial distress and unable to buy or pay. T12M DSO of 125d already exceeds the 90-day credit term by 35 days; with zero 2026 revenue, YTD DSO is undefined, and the effective collection urgency is uncapped. The longer the 60d+ bucket sits without resolution, the higher the probability it converts to bad debt — provisioning risk on SAR 1.13M is material.

On credit policy: expansion is ruled out because there is no volume to unlock (account has stopped purchasing entirely). Maintaining current exposure passively is also untenable — it signals tolerance of 62% OVD with no consequence. Credit must be formally reduced and new shipments suspended until the 60d+ balance is substantially cleared. The Grade A designation no longer reflects this account's collection behavior and should be reviewed against current Q3 Critical quadrant status before any future credit committee decision.
finance.credit_rationale: With 62% OVD, SAR 1.13M in 60d+ aging, and zero 2026 purchasing activity, maintaining current credit exposure without a payment milestone creates uncapped bad-debt risk entering the peak season.
action: Issue formal overdue demand notice for SAR 1,131,334 (60d+ bucket) with a 10-business-day cure deadline and explicit shipment-freeze consequence
Block all new orders and open order releases until minimum 50% of the 60d+ overdue is received and confirmed
Dispatch KAM within 5 business days to diagnose zero 2026 purchase root cause — financial distress, unresolved dispute, or strategic pause — and report back before any credit reinstatement discussion
finance.risk_reason: SAR 1.13M locked in 60d+ bucket, OVD flat at 62% with no payment trajectory, and zero 2026 revenue signal active financial stress entering peak season.

sales.headline: Alkhunaizan has fully disengaged from buying in 2026, is returning stock, and carries SAR 1.13M in severely aged overdue — the account is in a debt-trap freeze.
sales.analysis:
Channel health is effectively broken. The 2024→2025 sell-in surge (+67% YoY to SAR 10.1M) was aggressive relative to likely sell-out capacity in the OR channel. The 2026 net negative (-SAR 193K) is not a seasonal pause — it is net returns exceeding new purchases, signaling the account is offloading unsold inventory rather than replenishing. With zero new revenue YTD and a T12M DSO of 125 days against 90-day credit terms, the account has been functionally non-operative for at least the first four months of peak-season build. The most probable root cause sequence: over-purchase in 2025 → inventory overhang → slow sell-out → inability to pay down AR → credit line effectively blocked → buying freeze → returns to reduce liability exposure.

The AR picture reinforces the distress narrative. Of SAR 1.14M overdue, 99.2% (SAR 1.131M) is aged 60+ days — this is not a timing issue but a structural inability or unwillingness to pay. The OVD ratio has been locked at 62% for six consecutive daily snapshots with the balance barely moving (SAR 1.86M → 1.84M), meaning no meaningful cash collection is occurring. At this trajectory, the 60d+ bucket will continue to age into a write-off risk. The absence of any 30-60d bucket (SAR 0) confirms no new invoices have been raised in the relevant window — the account is completely static.

From an investment standpoint, any further credit extension or marketing co-investment would compound exposure with zero incremental sell-in to offset it. The only productive investment right now is time and relationship capital directed at a structured payment recovery. If LG can facilitate inventory clearance (consignment swap, trade-in assistance, merchandising to help the dealer move existing stock), it may unlock the cash flow the dealer needs to start servicing the overdue — which in turn reopens the ordering relationship before the May–September peak fully passes.
sales.investment_rationale: Recovering SAR 1.13M in aged overdue and diagnosing the sell-out blockage must precede any new credit or marketing commitment — investing further before this is resolved deepens LG's exposure with no volume offset.
action: Immediate credit hold enforcement: no new shipments until a written payment plan is signed for the SAR 1.131M 60d+ overdue balance

Executive-level visit within 7 days to diagnose sell-out situation, negotiate a 60/90-day repayment schedule, and assess whether inventory clearance support (consignment swap, display liquidation) can unlock payment capacity before peak season window closes

Conditional re-engagement path: define minimum payment milestone (e.g., SAR 500K cleared within 30 days) that would trigger partial credit reinstatement — communicate this clearly to the dealer as an incentive to settle, not a punitive measure
sales.channel_reason: Zero 2026 buy-in, net returns, 62% overdue locked for 6+ days with no collection movement — account is in full freeze ahead of peak season.

synthesis.headline: Alkhunaizan is in a debt-trap freeze: SAR 1.13M aged 60d+, zero 2026 purchases, net returns — every day without a signed payment plan is a day closer to a write-off entering peak season.

verdict.one_line: Risk RED — Hold. Recover SAR 1.13M before any new credit or investment; the account cannot buy until it pays, and the peak window is closing.

summary_3lines: Alkhunaizan aggressively loaded SAR 10.1M in 2025 (+67% YoY), could not sell through, and has been returning stock in 2026 — the account is structurally frozen: no new purchases, 62% of the SAR 1.84M balance aged 60d+, and zero payment movement for six consecutive days.

The root cause is a 2025 overstock trap: over-purchase outpaced sell-out capacity, inventory overhang blocked cash flow, AR aged past the credit term, and the account defaulted into a buying freeze rather than defaulting outright — a dealer managing its own insolvency risk at LG's expense.

If no action is taken this week, the SAR 1.13M 60d+ bucket continues aging toward write-off territory, the May–September peak passes without a single unit of new sell-in from a historically top-tier OR account, and the Grade A designation becomes a liability shield rather than a credit signal.
synthesis.decision_required: Commit this week to a structured payment recovery path with a defined re-engagement trigger — or escalate to formal legal demand. Both options require the same first move: executive contact within 7 days. The decision is whether the conversation opens with a partnership frame (payment plan + inventory clearance support) or a legal ultimatum.

options.option: Option A — Structured Recovery with Conditional Re-engagement
options.consequence: Offer a 60–90 day written payment plan (minimum SAR 500K cleared within 30 days as Phase 1 trigger) paired with LG inventory clearance support (consignment swap, display liquidation assistance). If milestone met, partial credit reinstated for peak season orders. Preserves the relationship and recovers most of the SAR 1.13M; risks delay if dealer cannot meet Phase 1 milestone.

options.option: Option B — Formal Demand Notice + Hard Freeze
options.consequence: Issue legal-grade overdue demand for SAR 1,131,334 with a 10-business-day cure deadline and explicit shipment suspension. No conditional re-engagement language — payment in full required before any credit discussion. Maximizes collection pressure and signals zero tolerance; high probability the trading relationship ends permanently and the account migrates to a competitor for the peak season.

timeline.step: Issue formal overdue notification (SAR 1,131,334) — written, signed, delivered. Block all open order releases immediately regardless of which option is chosen.
Executive + KAM joint visit: diagnose sell-out root cause, present Option A payment plan terms, assess dealer financial capacity, and obtain written commitment or go/no-go on Option B escalation.
Phase 1 payment milestone checkpoint: SAR 500K received and confirmed. If met — activate partial credit for peak season replenishment. If missed — escalate immediately to Option B legal proceedings.

finance.headline: S. Al.Bassam is effectively a non-performing account — SAR 11.86M entirely in 60d+ bucket, zero 2026 revenue, and six consecutive days of zero payment movement.
finance.analysis:
AR health is critically impaired. The entire SAR 11,859,454 balance sits in the 60d+ aging bucket with zero dollars in any fresher bucket, meaning there has been no payment activity whatsoever for an extended period. The OVD rate has been locked at 100% for at least six consecutive snapshots with no directional improvement — this is not a slow-pay account, this is a stalled account. The absence of any 0–30d or 30–60d portion confirms no partial payments are trickling in to roll the aging forward.

The revenue trajectory compounds the concern severely: SAR 4.84M in 2024 collapsed to SAR 629K in 2025 (an 87% YoY decline), and 2026 shows zero sell-in to date. This means LG has already informally tightened exposure — no new shipments have gone out — yet the legacy receivable of SAR 11.86M remains fully uncollected. With zero 2026 revenue, there is no DSO to calculate, which itself signals the account relationship has functionally ceased. The credit term is 90 days, but the entire balance is 60d+ overdue, implying actual days outstanding likely exceeds 150–180 days on the aged portion.

From a credit policy standpoint, there is no scenario that justifies maintaining or increasing the credit limit. The account carries a Grade D rating, sits in Q3 Critical quadrant, has demonstrated a multi-year revenue freefall, and has produced zero payment movement against a fully overdue balance heading into the May–September peak season. Extending additional credit here would increase LG's unsecured exposure with no evidence of either operational activity or payment intent. The credit limit must be reduced to zero (effective freeze) immediately, and any future engagement must be conditioned on full settlement or a bank-guaranteed payment plan.
finance.credit_rationale: 100% of SAR 11.86M balance is 60d+ overdue with zero 2026 revenue and no payment movement over six consecutive days — extending any credit would increase unrecoverable exposure.
action: Impose immediate credit hold: block all open orders and new shipments until overdue balance is cleared
Escalate SAR 11,859,454 to collections/legal team and demand a structured repayment schedule with binding commitment within 14 days
Initiate bad-debt provisioning review for the full balance given Grade D, Q3 Critical status and zero 2026 activity
finance.risk_reason: Entire balance 100% overdue in 60d+ bucket, zero 2026 revenue, no payment trend improvement, Grade D — maximum collection risk with no mitigating signals.

sales.headline: S. Al.Bassam has fully disengaged from LG in 2026 — zero buys during peak season entry while SAR 11.9M sits 100% overdue 60d+.
sales.analysis:
Channel health is in terminal decline. Sell-in collapsed 87% YoY (SAR 4.84M in 2024 → SAR 629K in 2025), and 2026 is a complete flatline with zero revenue through May 5 — the precise moment when any functioning OR account would be loading inventory ahead of the May–September peak. The absence of even a token purchase in 2026 is not a timing anomaly; it is a signal that the commercial relationship has effectively ceased. The working hypothesis is a 2024 over-buy event: the account likely took on large shipments in 2024 (nearly SAR 5M), failed to sell through at velocity, and is now sitting on both unsold stock and unpayable AR — a classic inventory-overhang-plus-cash-trap. The 60d+ concentration of 100% of the AR balance reinforces this: no installment payments, no partial settlements, no engagement. The account is not buying because it cannot, financially.

From an investment lens, there is nothing to unlock. Credit increase would compound the collection risk on an already fully overdue ledger. Sell-out support (promotions, display, installer subsidies) requires a functioning sell-out operation — with zero 2026 Sell-In, there is no channel activity to amplify. Co-marketing presupposes a partner who is actively selling; this account is not. The only commercially rational action is collection escalation. The SAR 11.86M exposure, if not recovered, represents a multiple of the account's entire 2025 revenue contribution and will permanently impair LG's OR P&L for this segment.

With peak season already opening (May), every week without collection action increases the probability of the debt aging into write-off territory. Grade D classification and Q3-Critical quadrant placement are appropriate — but the real risk is that without a structured recovery plan (legal notice, asset lien review, guarantor activation), this becomes a bad debt by Q3 end. No new investment is warranted until AR is settled in full.
sales.investment_rationale: 100% AR overdue 60d+ with zero 2026 Sell-In means there is no active channel to invest in — only SAR 11.9M to recover before further exposure accumulates.
action: Escalate to legal/credit team immediately: issue formal demand letter citing 60d+ overdue status and 90d credit term breach — set 14-day cure deadline
Activate guarantor or security instruments on file; engage LG KSA Finance to assess lien or asset recovery options before peak season closes the window
Hard-block all new shipments and credit extension until AR is settled in full; communicate block formally to account owner and sales team to prevent workaround orders
sales.channel_reason: Zero 2026 Sell-In during peak season entry + 100% AR overdue 60d+ = commercial relationship has ceased; collection is the only remaining objective.

synthesis.headline: SAR 11.86M is at acute write-off risk — zero payment movement, zero 2026 revenue, and peak season already opening. Legal escalation this week is the only lever with remaining time-value.

verdict.one_line: Risk RED — Exit. The commercial relationship has ceased; the only remaining objective is recovering SAR 11.86M before it ages into an unrecoverable bad debt.

summary_3lines: SAR 11,859,454 sits 100% in the 60d+ aging bucket with zero payment movement across six consecutive daily snapshots and zero sell-in in 2026 — the account has fully stopped.

The root cause is a 2024 over-buy event (SAR 4.84M) that produced unsold inventory overhang, a 2025 sell-through collapse (−87% YoY to SAR 629K), and a resulting cash trap that has made both payment and new purchases impossible.

If no legal action is taken this week, the debt ages deeper through peak season (May–September), guarantor and security instruments lose enforcement leverage, and the probability of full write-off by Q3 approaches certainty — permanently impairing OR P&L.
synthesis.decision_required: Authorize immediate legal escalation: issue formal demand letter citing 90-day credit term breach, activate all guarantor and security instruments on file, and impose a hard shipment block across all sales channels — decision required by 2026-05-09.

options.option: Option A — Immediate Legal Escalation (Recommended)
options.consequence: Formal demand letter served by May 9 with 14-day cure deadline; guarantor/security instruments activated in parallel; hard credit block enforced. Maximizes recovery probability while peak season leverage window is still open. Relationship is effectively already over — legal cost is low relative to SAR 11.86M at risk.

options.option: Option B — Final Direct Negotiation Before Legal
options.consequence: One structured meeting with account owner this week to negotiate a binding repayment schedule (e.g., 30% down + monthly installments with PDC cheques). Preserves a settlement path without litigation. Risk: costs 2–3 weeks of aging and signals LG hesitancy; account likely lacks liquidity to meet any schedule, making this a delay tactic with high probability of converging on Option A anyway.

timeline.step: Hard credit block confirmed to all sales staff and order management — no new shipments, no open-order processing, no exceptions
Formal demand letter issued to S. Al.Bassam citing 60d+ overdue breach of 90-day credit term; 14-day cure deadline set
Guarantor identification, security instrument review, and asset lien assessment initiated by LG KSA Finance
Bad-debt provisioning review initiated for full SAR 11.86M balance given Grade D / Q3-Critical status
If no payment commitment received by cure deadline — escalate to legal action and file formal claim

finance.headline: Black Box Co. carries zero overdue exposure today, but its T12M DSO of 240d reveals a chronic slow-pay pattern running 2.7× above the 90-day credit term — clean on the surface, structurally stretched underneath.
finance.analysis:
AR health on the snapshot date is unimpeachable: SAR 7.2M balance, 0% overdue across all aging buckets, and a steady OVD flatline through the past six days. The account earned its Grade A and Q1-Safe status. However, the DSO story beneath that surface demands a harder look. YTD DSO of 148 days already exceeds the 90-day credit term by 58 days, and the T12M DSO of 240 days — nearly 8 months — indicates this is not a 2026 anomaly but an entrenched payment behavior. The Q1-Safe classification uses LG's internal DPO (180 days) as the benchmark, not the contractual credit term (90 days); by the credit-term standard, Black Box is chronically late. The 1.6× buy-pace acceleration entering peak season means AR exposure will grow before collection cycles close, compressing the effective margin of safety.

On revenue trajectory, the picture is structurally weakening: Sell-In has declined 38% from 2024 to 2025 and is tracking for another year of contraction in 2026 (SAR 6.1M YTD vs SAR 11.1M full-year 2025). Sell-Out corroborates this — 4,181 units in 2025 vs 6,196 in 2024, with near-zero 2026 data this early in peak season. Whether the decline reflects genuine end-market softness, a shift in product mix, or competitive displacement (Samsung, Gree pricing pressure in OR channel) is not yet clear from this data alone. The channel inventory position is lean (only 13 units stock reported at end of 2025), which limits near-term Sell-Out risk but also signals reduced stocking commitment.

Collection risk is yellow, not red: the probability of actual write-off is low given Grade A history and current zero-OVD posture, but the DSO structure means LG is effectively extending 5–8 months of float well beyond contractual terms. As peak-season orders accumulate through May–September, the balance will swell before the T12M DSO pattern allows it to normalize — creating a temporary window of elevated concentration risk.
finance.credit_rationale: Zero current OVD and Grade A status justify holding existing exposure, but T12M DSO of 240d and a three-year declining revenue trajectory do not support expanding credit headroom until DSO normalizes toward the 90-day contractual term.
action: Issue a formal DSO remediation notice: communicate that YTD DSO of 148d and T12M DSO of 240d breach the 90-day credit term, and set a 60-day target to bring YTD DSO below 120d — document in writing before peak-season orders escalate.

Implement a peak-season balance cap: flag the account for manual credit review if the AR balance exceeds SAR 10M during May–September, given the 1.6× buy-pace acceleration and the risk of DSO drifting further during peak.

Investigate the Sell-In/Sell-Out divergence: 2025 Sell-Out dropped 33% YoY while 2026 Sell-In is accelerating — cross-check channel inventory levels, promotional commitments, and competitive pricing with the OR sales team before approving additional peak-season orders.
finance.risk_reason: Current AR is clean but T12M DSO of 240d (2.7× credit term) signals chronic slow-pay structurally, with peak-season exposure set to grow before collection cycles resolve.

sales.headline: Grade-A account aggressively restocking into peak season at 1.6x pace, but 2025 sell-out eroded -32% and 2026 POS shows zero units — inventory overhang risk is real before credit or promo investment is warranted.
sales.analysis:
Sell-in has been structurally declining for two consecutive years (SAR 17.9M 2024 → SAR 11.1M 2025, -38% YoY), yet 2026 YTD buy pace is running at 1.6x the T12M monthly average — the account is pre-loading inventory aggressively ahead of the May–Sep peak. This acceleration is a positive engagement signal, but it sits on a fragile demand foundation: 2025 consumer sell-out collapsed -32% (6,196u → 4,181u), with the flagship LO182C dropping from 1,716u to just 691u. The 2026 POS figure shows zero units — either a data reporting lag or genuinely flat consumer offtake, both of which require immediate field verification before any investment commitment. The model mix shift toward ND182C/ND242C in 2025 suggests the account took a SKU bet that underperformed, likely leaving residual stock that compressed 2025 buy-in. The pattern — heavy buy in 2024 → slow sell-out → compressed buy-in 2025 → now re-accelerating buy in 2026 — points to a recurring inventory overhang cycle.

AR is currently clean: 0% OVD across all aging buckets and a balance of SAR 7.2M reflecting recent buys. However, YTD DSO of 148d against a 90d credit term reveals a chronic structural slow-pay pattern — they consistently stretch 1.6x beyond agreed terms. The T12M DSO of 240d amplifies this concern. The Q1-Safe quadrant is technically correct given current OVD is below the 35.4% baseline, but the DSO profile means the account is effectively self-financing on LG's credit far beyond contractual limits. This is a latent risk that becomes acute the moment OVD ticks up.

Investment rationale: the bottleneck is not supply — the account is already buying heavily. The constraint is demand-side sell-out conversion. Extending credit now would amplify inventory overhang without solving the root problem, and the elevated DSO profile makes that doubly risky. The correct lever is sell-out activation: in-store display, installer co-op, and POS promotions tied to measurable offtake targets. If 2026 consumer velocity recovers to 2023–2024 levels (5,600–6,200u range) by Q2-end, the account earns a full investment upgrade. Until then, hold and activate demand.
sales.investment_rationale: Account is restocking into peak but 2026 consumer POS is unverified at zero and DSO is structurally 1.6–2.7x the credit term, making demand activation the priority over supply-side credit extension.
action: Field-verify 2026 sell-out immediately — confirm whether 0u is a data pipeline gap or actual zero POS velocity; escalate to OR KAM within 48 hours before next order cycle
Deploy sell-out support package (display refresh, certified installer referral, Q2 promotion) tied to a minimum POS target of 1,500u by end-June as a gate to full investment unlock
Set DSO monthly watch trigger at 160d YTD — current clean OVD is fragile given T12M baseline of 240d; any aging bucket opening should trigger immediate AR review before next shipment release
sales.channel_reason: Buy pace accelerating 1.6x into peak but 2025 sell-out eroded -32% and 2026 POS is silent — inventory conversion risk elevated until field verification confirms consumer velocity.

synthesis.headline: Clean AR masks a chronic DSO problem and a stalling sell-out cycle — the 1.6× buy surge entering peak season is a warning flag, not a green light, until 2026 POS is field-verified and DSO remediation is documented.

verdict.one_line: Risk Yellow — Hold. Grade A with zero OVD today, but T12M DSO 240d (2.7× credit term) and unverified 2026 POS signal an inventory overhang cycle repeating before any credit or promotional investment is extended.

summary_3lines: Black Box is pre-loading at 1.6× pace into peak season with a clean 0% OVD balance — but beneath the surface it carries a T12M DSO of 240d, a sell-out trajectory down 32% YoY in 2025, and zero confirmed 2026 POS.

The same cycle is repeating: 2024 heavy buy-in → 2025 slow sell-out and inventory overhang → 38% Sell-In collapse; 2026 is now recapitulating the setup with no evidence yet that consumer velocity has recovered.

If 2026 POS is genuinely flat and the buy acceleration continues unchecked, AR will swell past SAR 10M through peak season on an unchanged DSO structure — and when overdue eventually opens (as T12M history predicts), collection leverage will be weakest exactly when balance is highest.
synthesis.decision_required: Confirm within 48 hours whether 2026 POS = 0u is a data pipeline gap or actual zero consumer offtake — this single data point determines whether the 1.6× buy acceleration is demand-driven restocking or another inventory cycle loading onto LG's credit before the overhang is cleared.

options.option: Option A — Extend credit and invest now without waiting for POS verification
options.consequence: Captures full peak-season buy momentum from a Grade A account. But if 2026 POS is genuinely flat, LG absorbs 5–8 months of float on a growing balance with no demand-pull backstop — replicating the 2025 cycle that produced the 38% Sell-In collapse entering 2026.

options.option: Option B — Hold investment, activate demand first, gate on verifiable POS milestone
options.consequence: Demand activation (display refresh, installer co-op, Q2 promotion) tied to 1,500u POS target by end-June breaks the overhang cycle and puts formal DSO remediation on paper before the balance grows further. Account may marginally slow order pace, but current 1.6× self-funded buy momentum makes this a manageable risk.

timeline.step: OR KAM field-verify 2026 POS: determine if 0u is a data lag or genuine zero consumer velocity — escalate to KAM by EOD and resolve before next order cycle

Issue formal DSO remediation notice: document in writing that YTD DSO 148d and T12M DSO 240d breach the 90d credit term; set written target of YTD DSO ≤120d within 60 days; make this a condition precedent for any peak-season credit limit expansion

Activate sell-out support package tied to 1,500u POS gate by end-June; schedule formal investment-upgrade review at that milestone — if POS target is met and DSO is trending down, escalate to Invest signal

finance.headline: 14% OVD spike is a mechanical calendar trigger, not a payment failure — all overdue is fresh 0-30d and below historical average; T12M DSO of 230d is the structural watch item entering peak season.
finance.analysis: AR health is better than the headline figure suggests. The SAR 704,867 overdue balance emerged entirely on May 1 as invoices crossed their due date, with zero in the 30-60d bucket and a marginal SAR -1,646 in 60d+ (indicative of prior overpayment credit). Current OVD of 14.3% sits well below the account's own YTD average baseline of 35.4%, meaning 2026 payment discipline is tracking better than historical norms. The clean aging profile on an A-grade account with this profile does not indicate collection stress. Collection risk is low-to-moderate. The near-term risk vector is manageable: freshly due invoices on an A-grade Q1 account typically clear within 2-3 weeks absent a deterioration signal. The structural concern is T12M DSO at 230d — 50 days above the DPO ceiling of 180d — which reflects a persistently slow baseline payment pattern. YTD DSO of 136d shows meaningful improvement and suggests the account is trending in the right direction for 2026, but the T12M figure cannot be dismissed heading into the May-September peak when balance exposure will expand further. The 1.7x buy-pace acceleration (SAR 4.5M already booked through early May) amplifies AR exposure risk if payment velocity does not keep pace. Credit policy should be maintained at current limits. The commercial case for an increase is partially supported — A-grade status, Q1 Safe quadrant, improving OVD trend, and a 1.7x buy acceleration signal an account preparing for peak season that may be capacity-constrained. However, T12M DSO of 230d above DPO is a structural disqualifier for immediate expansion. The correct sequence is: allow current 0-30d overdue to resolve, monitor whether June DSO tracks toward the 90d credit term trajectory, then reassess for a targeted limit increase before the July-August peak demand apex.
finance.credit_rationale: A-grade with improving 2026 OVD and strong buy momentum, but T12M DSO 230d above DPO 180d requires the current 0-30d overdue to clear before any limit expansion — June review warranted if collections normalize.
action: Contact Bin Momen AR team within 48 hours: confirm the SAR 704,867 May 1 overdue invoices and obtain a firm settlement ETA — target clearance by May 16
Set T12M DSO 230d watch flag: escalate internally if 0-30d overdue rolls into 30-60d bucket by May 31, or if new shipments are requested before current overdue is settled
Schedule June 1 credit limit review: if 0-30d clears and YTD DSO trends below 150d by end of May, prepare a credit increase proposal to support peak season Q3 volume ramp
finance.risk_reason: T12M DSO 230d structurally above 180d DPO; current OVD is calendar-triggered and below historical average, but trailing payment pattern requires active monitoring as peak season exposure builds

sales.headline: A-grade IR account accelerating into peak season at 1.7x buy pace with OVD well below baseline — but 2-year sell-in erosion of 53% demands root cause clarity before scaling investment.
sales.analysis:
Bin Momen's sell-in contraction from SAR 9.7M (2024) to SAR 6.7M (2025) — a 31% drop — is the structural question this account cannot answer without a field conversation. The hypotheses are not equivalent in investment implication: competitor displacement (Gree/Midea on price in IR) suggests LG needs sell-out firepower to recapture floor share; financial stress suggests tightening credit; portfolio rationalization suggests a focused SKU push. The 2026 YTD buy pace at 1.7x T12M average entering May is an unambiguously positive counter-signal — the account is pre-stocking aggressively, which typically reflects sell-out confidence or deliberate peak-season positioning. The balance jump from SAR 4.05M (Apr 29) to SAR 4.92M (Apr 30) confirms a material purchase tranche was placed on the eve of peak season.

OVD trajectory is the most encouraging data point: current 14.4% is dramatically below the account's own YTD baseline of 35.4%. The May 1 spike is calendar-mechanical — 90d credit term boundary triggering on the April 30 shipment — not a behavioral deterioration. The -SAR 1,646 in the 60d+ bucket is almost certainly a credit note, not a collection gap. The account is paying while buying more, a healthy co-movement. DSO remains the structural caution: YTD 136d against 90d terms, T12M 230d historically. This is a slow payer by nature, but current behavior is tracking well below their own norm.

The investment case is peak-season timing: May is the entry point for the Saudi summer cycle (May–September). An A-grade IR account loading inventory at 1.7x pace is the ideal vehicle for sell-out activation — promotional display, installer incentives, and co-op activity convert pre-loaded stock into sell-through velocity, which in turn funds payment cycles and reorder momentum. Without sell-out support now, the risk is an inventory overhang by July that forces margin-dilutive promotions with no LG benefit. Gate the investment on collection behavior: OVD staying below 20% through June is the qualifying condition for next tranche.
sales.investment_rationale: Peak-season pre-stocking at 1.7x pace in an A-grade IR account warrants immediate sell-out activation to convert loaded inventory into sell-through — gated on OVD remaining below 20% through June.
action: Deploy sell-out support package (POP display + top-3 SKU installer incentive) by May 12 — condition precedent: confirm OVD stays below 20% at next weekly AR snapshot

Schedule field visit week of May 12 to audit floor inventory mix and identify slow-moving SKUs before they become July overhang — specifically check Window vs Split ratio against Gree/Midea competitive placement
Set formal credit gate: if 30d OVD breaches 25% by end-May, pause next shipment pending partial settlement — communicate the gate to account manager now so it is not a surprise
sales.channel_reason: 1.7x buy-pace acceleration and OVD well below account baseline are positive, but 2-year sell-in erosion (-53% from 2024 peak) and T12M DSO of 230d leave recovery trajectory unconfirmed

synthesis.headline: A-grade IR account loading peak-season inventory at 1.7x pace with OVD well below its own baseline — invest now with a hard credit gate, but demand root-cause clarity on 53% two-year sell-in erosion before any limit expansion.

verdict.one_line: Risk YELLOW — Invest (gated). OVD spike is calendar mechanics, not collection failure, but T12M DSO 230d and unexplained 2-year erosion require a conditional, actively monitored commitment.

summary_3lines: Bin Momen is pre-stocking aggressively at 1.7x buy pace entering the May–September peak, with current OVD at 14.3% — well below its own 35.4% YTD baseline — signaling an account actively positioned to sell through.

The May 1 OVD spike is a mechanical 90-day calendar trigger on the April 30 shipment tranche, not behavioral deterioration; however, T12M DSO of 230d and a 53% sell-in contraction since the 2024 peak remain structurally unexplained and are the two risks that could make this account a trap rather than an opportunity.

Without sell-out activation this week, pre-loaded inventory risks becoming a margin-dilutive July overhang with no LG benefit; without a credit gate now, peak-season AR exposure compounds an already-slow T12M payment baseline with no mechanism to stop it.
synthesis.decision_required: Approve gated sell-out support deployment (POP display + top-3 SKU installer incentive) by May 12 — explicitly contingent on OVD staying at or below 20% at the next weekly AR snapshot — while simultaneously dispatching a field visit to audit floor inventory and diagnose the 2-year sell-in erosion root cause before any further credit conversation.

options.option: Option A — Invest (gated): deploy sell-out support by May 12, field visit same week, June 1 credit review
options.consequence: Converts pre-loaded peak-season stock into sell-through velocity; surfaces erosion root cause before committing further credit; if OVD clears and YTD DSO trends toward 150d by May-end, positions the account for a targeted credit increase ahead of the July–August demand apex — maximizing peak season capture with downside protection built in.

options.option: Option B — Hold: freeze all investment and new shipments until T12M DSO normalizes and erosion root cause is confirmed
options.consequence: Misses the peak-season activation window entirely; pre-loaded inventory overhangs without LG sell-out support, likely forcing margin-dilutive promotions that benefit competitors; account relationship cools at exactly the moment Gree and Midea are competing for IR floor share, and 2026 sell-in risks continuing the 2-year erosion trend.

timeline.step: AR contact: call Bin Momen accounts team, confirm SAR 704,867 May 1 overdue invoices and obtain firm settlement ETA — target clearance by May 16
Weekly AR snapshot gate check: if OVD remains at or below 20%, green-light sell-out support deployment (POP + installer incentive); if OVD exceeds 20%, hold deployment and escalate internally

Field visit: audit floor inventory mix (Window vs Split ratio), map Gree and Midea competitive placement, and interview account principal on the 2024→2025 sell-in contraction — this is the single most important unknown
